edge: Zero-shot quantization (ZSQ) is a method for compressing and accelerating deep neural networks, particularly when full-precision models are inaccessible.
In Zero-shot quantization (ZSQ), network quantization is performed using synthetic samples, and the quality of synthetic samples heavily influences the performance of quantized models.
Quantized models obtained using existing ZSQ methods suffer from performance degradation on hard samples due to the easy fit of synthetic samples by the models.
The proposed HArd sample Synthesizing and Training (HAST) method seeks to address the issue of performance degradation in quantized models compared to existing ZSQ methods.
HAST aligns features extracted by full-precision and quantized models in order to ensure similarity between the features extracted by these two models.

The methodology section introduces the process of zero-shot quantization (ZSQ) and details the proposed HArd sample Synthesizing and Training (HAST) scheme.

The HArd sample Synthesizing and Training (HAST) scheme contains three parts: hard sample synthesis, sample difficulty promotion, and feature alignment ensuring the similarity of representations from quantized models and full precision models.
The overview of HAST is illustrated in Figure 3.3.1.
The focus is on the asymmetric uniform quantizer to implement network quantization.
The quantizer produces the quantized integer θq according to specific calculations.
The quantized integer θq is mapped to a quantized value zero.
The corresponding dequantized value can be calculated based on the quantized integer θq.
The weights of full-precision models can be represented using low-bit integers.

The dequantized parameter θ′ is typically paired with considerable performance degradation.
To mitigate the performance degradation, a widely adopted approach is to optimize θ such that its dequantized parameter θ′ can perform well on test sets.
This is typically achieved by optimizing θ using the data for training full-precision models, since θ′ is inherently the function of θ.
Zero-Shot Quantization (ZSQ) takes a step further to quantize models when data for training full-precision models are inaccessible.

Sample difficulty promotion process is explained to be different from hard sample synthesis process.
Full-precision models are used to synthesize samples for training quantized models.
Quantized models are used to enhance the sample difficulty for training themselves in the sample difficulty promotion process.
If synthetic samples are changed in the process of sample difficulty promotion, they would be both easy and difficult for quantized models.
Inspiration is drawn from adversarial training, where samples are modified to be hard to fit on the fly.
Sample difficulty d(xi, θ') is increased on the fly to make synthetic samples hard to fit by introducing a perturbation δi for xi in each training iteration.
A perturbation δi is introduced for xi in each training iteration, with the perturbation attached to find nearby difficult samples with larger difficulty in each iteration.
The perturbation δi is attached to find nearby difficult samples with larger sample difficulty in each iteration.
The nearby difficult samples with larger sample difficulty are found using the perturbation δi in each iteration.
The proposed hard sample synthesis and sample difficulty promotion are built upon, to train quantized models using hard samples.

To ensure the quantized model performs the same as its full-precision model, the outputs and intermediate features of both models are kept exactly the same, aligning the features of the full-precision and quantized models.
The quantized models may not be able to match the outputs of full-precision models due to limited model capacity.
The features of the quantized model are aligned with the features of the full-precision model to minimize the quantization gap.

The loss function Eq. (12) is used for fine-tuning the quantized models by SGD with Nesterov. Both equations involve hyper-parameters β and α for fair comparison.
5120 images are synthesized following the settings of IntraQ for dataset preparation. Comparison results show significant improvements on CIFAR-10/100 with our method achieving higher accuracy.
Our method increases the top-1 accuracy of 3-bit quantized models by 7.85% on CIFAR-10 and 7.03% on CIFAR-100 compared to AIT.
IntraQ achieved 3-bit accuracy of 77.07% and 48.25% on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 respectively.

The proposed method reaches 88.34% performance on CIFAR-10.
The proposed method reaches 55.67% performance on CIFAR-100.

The fine-tuning process of MobileNetV2 is unstable and shows that the training process has a greater impact on performance recovery than the sample difficulty.
AIT performs better than HAST on MobileNetV2 due to the dynamic learning rate for each convolution kernel.
AIT can be used in combination with HAST as long as memory and time overheads allow.
Experiments show that the synthetic data generated by the proposed HSAT scheme significantly improves the performance of the quantized model.
Synthesizing and learning hard samples is important for improving the quantized model, especially when the model is quantized to lower bit-width.
The effectiveness of the proposed hard sample synthesis is studied to improve performance.
These two concepts are discussed in Section 3.3 and Section 3.4 respectively, highlighting the importance of synthesizing hard samples and promoting sample difficulty in the experimental process.
In Section 3.4 and Section 3.5, the relationship between sample difficulty promotion and feature alignment is discussed, showcasing their interdependency in improving accuracy.
The addition of adversarial augmentation as a component for synthesizing hard samples results in a high accuracy improvement of 3.79%, emphasizing the effectiveness of this technique.

Attention transfer aids the quantized model in acquiring informative knowledge from the intermediate features of the full-precision model, leading to performance enhancement by 1.39% in the absence of hard samples.
The significance of hard samples in enhancing the training of quantized models is highlighted, as evidenced by the performance improvement obtained when using them during synthesis and learning.

The combination of hard sample synthesis and feature alignment, as discussed in Section 3.3 and Section 3.5 respectively, contributes to achieving the best performance of 51.15% when both components are applied.

Through the ablations performed on different components, such as hard sample synthesis and sample difficulty promotion, the experimental results demonstrate the impact of these techniques on accuracy improvement.
